questions.question: What is the 'Trigger' in a marketing automation workflow?

options.text: The email that is sent to the customer.
The event that starts the entire workflow.
The delay between two emails.
The button the founder clicks to stop the system.

questions.explanation: A trigger is the 'When X happens' part of an automation—the event that signifies a workflow should begin.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an 'Action' in an automation sequence?

options.text: A user visiting the pricing page.
A subscriber opening an email.
Sending a Slack notification to the founder.
A lead score reaching a threshold of 50.

questions.explanation: Actions are what the system actually *does* (e.g., sending an email, adding a tag, or sending a notification) in response to a trigger.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using 'Conditional Logic' (If/Then branches) in your automation?

options.text: It makes the automation run faster.
It ensures everyone receives exactly the same experience to maintain brand consistency.
It allows you to personlize the experience based on user behavior without manual work.
It reduces the cost of the automation tool.

questions.explanation: Conditional logic allows you to branch workflows so that engaged leads get different content than unengaged leads, enabling personalization at scale.

questions.question: In an 'Engagement Re-activator' workflow, what is the typical trigger?

options.text: A new email subscription.
No email opens for a specified period (e.g., 60-90 days).
A purchase completion.
A visit to the pricing page.

questions.explanation: Re-activation workflows target dormant subscribers who haven't engaged recently, trying to bring them back or clean them from the list.

questions.question: Why does the lesson recommend placing your automation 'brain' where your data lives?

options.text: To save on monthly subscription costs.
To avoid complex 'glue' tools like Zapier for basic functions and reduce failure points.
Because most tools don't allow external integrations.
To ensure the AI coach can't access your data.

questions.explanation: Using native automation within your CRM or email tool is generally more reliable and easier to manage than chaining multiple external tools for simple tasks.

questions.question: Draft the logic for one 'If/Then' branch in your welcome sequence. What behavior will trigger the branch (e.g., clicking a specific case study)? What will the 'Yes' path do? What will the 'No' path do? How does this branch help qualify the lead?
questions.promptForAI: Evaluate the student's branching logic. Ensure the 'Yes' and 'No' actions are logically distinct and serve a qualification purpose. Suggest one 'Action' they could automate on the 'Yes' path to notify themselves of a high-interest lead.
